title: Expect 100-continue, Early Responses, Body Rejection, and Draining · Go Semantics Case: Client Continue Timeout
summary: A server reads a huge body before authentication, sends 100 after a final response, drains attacker bytes without bounds, and lets client and server wait on each other forever. Planned artifact: Add and verify the client continue timeout increment in expectation and early-response coordination state machine. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Bound client waiting for 100, handle early final responses, and avoid sending content after rejection.
Send 100 Continue only after header-level policy accepts receiving content and before a final response commits.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Predict Client Continue Timeout · Theory Client Continue Timeout
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory protocol team handles case 4259 in a signed webhook receiver behind an HTTP/2 to HTTP/1.1 gateway. Build deterministic Go evidence to coordinate 100-continue and bounded early rejection without deadlock or unsafe reuse; preserve this operating constraint: all attacker-controlled counts must be validated before allocation or slicing; then force a short write and retain the exact transmitted prefix plus remaining count. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.
